title: joinStringsWith, type of partial, volume
prompt:
From the exercises in FPIL §1.3 (Functions and Definitions):

1. Define joinStringsWith with type String → String → String → String that creates a new string by placing its first argument between its second and third arguments. joinStringsWith ", " "one" "and another" should evaluate to "one, and another".

2. What is the type of joinStringsWith ": "? Check your answer with Lean.

3. Define a function volume with type Nat → Nat → Nat → Nat that computes the volume of a rectangular prism with the given height, width, and depth.
starterCode:
-- 1.
def joinStringsWith (sep s1 s2 : String) : String :=
  sorry

#eval joinStringsWith ", " "one" "and another"
-- expect: "one, and another"

-- 2. Predict the type, then check
#check joinStringsWith ": "

-- 3.
def volume (h w d : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

#eval volume 2 3 4  -- expect: 24

title: Inspect dependent types with #check
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 2 (Dependent Type Theory). This chapter introduces the foundations but has no formal Exercises section; the practice below uses #check and #eval the way the chapter does, on definitions you write.

1. Define a polymorphic identity function id' with type {α : Type} → α → α and check its type with #check.
2. Define const : {α β : Type} → α → β → α that ignores its second argument.
3. Use #eval to apply both to a few different argument types.
starterCode:
def id' {α : Type} (a : α) : α :=
  sorry

def const {α β : Type} (a : α) (_ : β) : α :=
  sorry

#check @id'
#check @const

#eval id' 5
#eval id' "lean"
#eval const 7 "ignored"

title: RectangularPrism and Segment
prompt:
From the exercises in FPIL §1.4 (Structures):

1. Define a structure named RectangularPrism that contains the height, width, and depth of a rectangular prism, each as a Float.

2. Define a function named volume : RectangularPrism → Float that computes the volume of a rectangular prism.

3. Define a structure named Segment that represents a line segment by its endpoints, and define a function length : Segment → Float that computes the length of a line segment. Segment should have at most two fields.
starterCode:
structure RectangularPrism where
  height : Float
  width  : Float
  depth  : Float
deriving Repr

def volume (p : RectangularPrism) : Float :=
  sorry

#eval volume { height := 2.0, width := 3.0, depth := 4.0 }   -- expect: 24.0

structure Point where
  x : Float
  y : Float
deriving Repr

structure Segment where
  start  : Point
  finish : Point
deriving Repr

def length (s : Segment) : Float :=
  sorry

#eval length { start := ⟨0, 0⟩, finish := ⟨3, 4⟩ }   -- expect: 5.0

title: Identities of propositional logic
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of TPIL Ch. 3 (Propositions and Proofs):

Prove the following identities, replacing the sorry placeholders with actual proofs.
starterCode:
variable (p q r : Prop)

-- commutativity of ∧ and ∨
example : p ∧ q ↔ q ∧ p := sorry
example : p ∨ q ↔ q ∨ p := sorry

-- associativity of ∧ and ∨
example : (p ∧ q) ∧ r ↔ p ∧ (q ∧ r) := sorry
example : (p ∨ q) ∨ r ↔ p ∨ (q ∨ r) := sorry

-- distributivity
example : p ∧ (q ∨ r) ↔ (p ∧ q) ∨ (p ∧ r) := sorry
example : p ∨ (q ∧ r) ↔ (p ∨ q) ∧ (p ∨ r) := sorry

-- the book lists more — keep going if you want the full set:
-- example : (p → (q → r)) ↔ (p ∧ q → r) := sorry
-- example : ((p ∨ q) → r) ↔ (p → r) ∧ (q → r) := sorry
-- example : ¬(p ∨ q) ↔ ¬p ∧ ¬q := sorry

title: Echo a single line of stdin
prompt:
Adapted from FPIL Ch. 2 (Hello, World!). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below mirrors the chapter's running examples (the `hello` and `feline` programs).

Write a program echo that reads one line from standard input, then prints it back with a trailing newline. Use IO.getStdin / IO.FS.Stream.getLine for input and IO.println for output.
starterCode:
def echo : IO Unit := do
  let stdin ← IO.getStdin
  let line ← stdin.getLine
  sorry

#eval echo  -- in the Lean editor, this will prompt for one line

title: Quantifier identities
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of TPIL Ch. 4 (Quantifiers and Equality):

Prove these equivalences. The reverse direction of the last one is not derivable — try to understand why.
starterCode:
variable (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop)

example : (∀ x, p x ∧ q x) ↔ (∀ x, p x) ∧ (∀ x, q x) := sorry
example : (∀ x, p x → q x) → (∀ x, p x) → (∀ x, q x) := sorry
example : (∀ x, p x) ∨ (∀ x, q x) → ∀ x, p x ∨ q x := sorry

-- Bonus from the book: the barber paradox is a contradiction.
example (men : Type) (barber : men) (shaves : men → men → Prop)
    (h : ∀ x : men, shaves barber x ↔ ¬ shaves x x) : False :=
  sorry

title: One-line tactic combinator proof
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of TPIL Ch. 5 (Tactics):

Use tactic combinators (the ones introduced in the chapter — repeat, all_goals, <;>, first, etc.) to obtain a one-line proof of the following:
starterCode:
example (p q r : Prop) (hp : p)
    : (p ∨ q ∨ r) ∧ (q ∨ p ∨ r) ∧ (q ∨ r ∨ p) := by
  sorry

-- The book also says: "Go back to the exercises in Propositions and Proofs
-- and Quantifiers and Equality and redo as many as you can now with tactic
-- proofs, using also `rw` and `simp` as appropriate."

title: Pos: Add, Mul, ToString, OfNat
prompt:
From the exercises in FPIL §4 (Type Classes), the "Another Representation" exercise from the Positive Numbers section:

Replace Pos with a structure whose constructor is named succ that contains a Nat (so Pos.succ 0 is 1, Pos.succ 1 is 2, etc.). Define instances of Add, Mul, ToString, and OfNat that let this version of Pos be used conveniently.
starterCode:
structure Pos where
  succ ::
  pred : Nat

instance : Add Pos where
  add a b := sorry

instance : Mul Pos where
  mul a b := sorry

instance : ToString Pos where
  toString p := sorry

instance : OfNat Pos (n + 1) where
  ofNat := sorry

#eval (7 : Pos) + (3 : Pos)
#eval (toString ((7 : Pos) * (3 : Pos)))

title: Inspect a definition with #check, #eval, #print, #reduce
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 6 (Interacting with Lean). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below uses the introspection commands the chapter introduces.

1. Define add1 : Nat → Nat that returns its argument plus one.
2. #check it — note the difference between #check add1 and #check @add1.
3. #eval it on a small input.
4. #print its body to see what Lean stored.
5. #reduce a small application like add1 (add1 0).
starterCode:
def add1 (n : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

#check add1            -- elaborated type with implicit args hidden
#check @add1           -- with implicits made explicit
#eval add1 41          -- expect: 42
#print add1            -- the actual term Lean stored
#reduce add1 (add1 0)  -- expect: 2

title: BinTree.mapM
prompt:
From the exercises in FPIL Ch. 5 (Monads), the "Mapping on a Tree" exercise:

By analogy to mapM for lists, define BinTree.mapM that applies a monadic function to each data entry in a binary tree as a preorder traversal. The required type signature is:

  def BinTree.mapM [Monad m] (f : α → m β) : BinTree α → m (BinTree β)

The book also asks you to argue why a specific bad Option monad instance (where bind always returns none) violates the monad contract.
starterCode:
inductive BinTree (α : Type) where
  | leaf
  | branch (l : BinTree α) (a : α) (r : BinTree α)
deriving Repr

def BinTree.mapM [Monad m] (f : α → m β) : BinTree α → m (BinTree β)
  | .leaf         => sorry
  | .branch l a r => sorry

-- Bonus: explain why this instance breaks the monad contract.
-- instance : Monad Option where
--   pure x := some x
--   bind opt next := none

title: Operations on the natural numbers
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of TPIL Ch. 7 (Inductive Types):

Try defining other operations on the natural numbers, such as multiplication, the predecessor function (with pred 0 = 0), truncated subtraction (with n - m = 0 when m is greater than or equal to n), and exponentiation. Then try proving some of their basic properties, building on the theorems already proved in the chapter.

Since many of these are already defined in Lean's core library, work within a namespace named Hidden to avoid name clashes.
starterCode:
namespace Hidden

def mul (m n : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

def pred (n : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

def sub (m n : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

def exp (m n : Nat) : Nat :=
  sorry

#eval mul 6 7        -- expect: 42
#eval pred 0         -- expect: 0
#eval pred 5         -- expect: 4
#eval sub 3 10       -- expect: 0
#eval exp 2 10       -- expect: 1024

end Hidden

title: Functor instance for a binary tree
prompt:
From the exercises in FPIL §4 (Standard Classes), which the chapter on Functors / Applicatives / Monads builds on:

Implement a Functor instance for the binary tree datatype.

(FPIL's dedicated FAM chapter exercises are mostly pencil-and-paper proofs about the monad contract; this is the closest code-writing exercise in the same domain.)
starterCode:
inductive BinTree (α : Type) where
  | leaf
  | branch (l : BinTree α) (a : α) (r : BinTree α)
deriving Repr

instance : Functor BinTree where
  map f t := sorry

def sample : BinTree Nat :=
  .branch (.branch .leaf 1 .leaf) 2 (.branch .leaf 3 .leaf)

#eval (· * 10) <$> sample
-- each entry multiplied by 10

title: add, mul, exp via the equation compiler
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of TPIL Ch. 8 (Induction and Recursion):

Open a namespace Hidden to avoid naming conflicts, and use the equation compiler to define addition, multiplication, and exponentiation on the natural numbers. Then use the equation compiler to derive some of their basic properties.
starterCode:
namespace Hidden

def add : Nat → Nat → Nat
  | m, .zero   => sorry
  | m, .succ n => sorry

def mul : Nat → Nat → Nat
  | _, .zero   => sorry
  | m, .succ n => sorry

def exp : Nat → Nat → Nat
  | _, .zero   => sorry
  | m, .succ n => sorry

#eval add 3 4    -- expect: 7
#eval mul 6 7    -- expect: 42
#eval exp 2 10   -- expect: 1024

-- Now try proving something with `induction` or pattern-matching on Nat,
-- e.g.   theorem add_zero (n : Nat) : add n 0 = n := sorry

end Hidden

title: Point and Point3D via extends
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 9 (Structures and Records). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below echoes the chapter's running examples on inheritance via extends.

1. Define a Point structure with two Float fields x and y.
2. Define Point3D that extends Point with a Float field z.
3. Write lift : Point → Point3D that puts the point in the z = 0 plane.
4. Write a function that drops a Point3D back to a Point (forgets z).

title: Inhabited and decide on a small inductive
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 10 (Type Classes). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below mirrors the chapter's worked examples on Inhabited and Decidable.

1. Define a small inductive Color with red, green, blue.
2. Give it an Inhabited instance picking your favourite default.
3. Derive DecidableEq for it and use decide to evaluate a (Color = Color) check.
starterCode:
inductive Color where
  | red
  | green
  | blue
deriving Repr, DecidableEq

instance : Inhabited Color where
  default := sorry

#eval (default : Color)

example : (Color.red = Color.red) = True := by decide
example : (Color.red = Color.green) = False := by decide

title: Rewrite a subterm with conv
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 11 (The Conversion Tactic). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below uses the same conv patterns the chapter demonstrates.

Use conv to navigate into a subterm and rewrite it. The trick is to descend with conv_lhs / conv_rhs (or with conv in <pattern>) so that rw applies only inside the part you want to change.
starterCode:
example (a b c : Nat) (h : a = b) : c + a = c + b := by
  conv_rhs => rw [← h]

example (f : Nat → Nat) (a b : Nat) (h : a = b) : f a + 1 = f b + 1 := by
  sorry

example (a b : Nat) (h : a = b) : (fun x => x + a) 0 = b := by
  sorry

title: Two functions equal pointwise via funext
prompt:
Adapted from TPIL Ch. 12 (Axioms and Computation). This chapter has no formal Exercises section; the practice below mirrors the chapter's demonstration of funext (function extensionality).

1. Recall the type of funext with #check.
2. Use funext to prove (fun x : Nat => x + 0) equals (fun x => x).
3. Use funext to prove (fun x : Nat => x + 1) equals (Nat.succ).
starterCode:
#check @funext

example : (fun x : Nat => x + 0) = (fun x => x) := by
  funext x
  sorry

example : (fun x : Nat => x + 1) = Nat.succ := by
  funext x
  sorry

title: Logging Transformer
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of FPIL Ch. 6, Monad Transformers section:

Define a monad-transformer version of WithLog. Also define the corresponding type class MonadWithLog, and write a program that combines logging and exceptions.

WithLog from the chapter is roughly:

  structure WithLog (logged α) where
    log    : List logged
    result : α
starterCode:
structure WithLogT (logged : Type) (m : Type → Type) (α : Type) where
  run : m (List logged × α)

instance [Monad m] : Monad (WithLogT logged m) where
  pure a := sorry
  bind ma f := sorry

class MonadWithLog (logged : outParam Type) (m : Type → Type) where
  saveLog : logged → m Unit

instance [Monad m] : MonadWithLog logged (WithLogT logged m) where
  saveLog l := sorry

-- Now compose logging with exceptions and try running a program:
abbrev LE α := WithLogT String (Except String) α

def demo : LE Nat := sorry

title: Vect operations: map, zipWith, push, reverse
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of FPIL Ch. 7 (Indexed Families), abbreviated:

Getting a feel for programming with dependent types requires experience. For each function below, see which mistakes the type checker can catch by experimenting.

• Define Vect.map  : (α → β) → Vect α n → Vect β n
• Define Vect.zipWith : (α → β → γ) → Vect α n → Vect β n → Vect γ n
• Define Vect.push : Vect α n → α → Vect α (n + 1)
• Define Vect.reverse : Vect α n → Vect α n
starterCode:
inductive Vect (α : Type) : Nat → Type where
  | nil  : Vect α 0
  | cons : α → Vect α n → Vect α (n + 1)
deriving Repr

def Vect.map (f : α → β) : Vect α n → Vect β n
  | .nil       => sorry
  | .cons x xs => sorry

def Vect.zipWith (f : α → β → γ) : Vect α n → Vect β n → Vect γ n
  | .nil,       .nil       => sorry
  | .cons x xs, .cons y ys => sorry

def Vect.push : Vect α n → α → Vect α (n + 1)
  | .nil,       y => sorry
  | .cons x xs, y => sorry

def Vect.reverse : Vect α n → Vect α n :=
  sorry  -- tip: an accumulator helper will need a cleverer type signature

#eval (Vect.cons 1 (.cons 2 (.cons 3 .nil))).map (· * 10)
#eval (Vect.cons 1 (.cons 2 .nil)).push 99

title: length, factorial, filter — to tail-recursive
prompt:
From the exercises at the end of FPIL Ch. 8 (Tail Recursion):

Translate each of the following non-tail-recursive functions into accumulator-passing tail-recursive functions. The translation of filter should result in a program that takes constant stack space through tail recursion and time linear in the length of the input list — a constant factor overhead is acceptable relative to the original.
starterCode:
namespace NonTail

def length : List α → Nat
  | []      => 0
  | _ :: xs => length xs + 1

def factorial : Nat → Nat
  | 0     => 1
  | n + 1 => factorial n * (n + 1)

def filter (p : α → Bool) : List α → List α
  | []      => []
  | x :: xs => if p x then x :: filter p xs else filter p xs

end NonTail

namespace Tail

def length (xs : List α) : Nat :=
  let rec go : List α → Nat → Nat
    | [],      acc => sorry
    | _ :: xs, acc => sorry
  go xs 0

def factorial (n : Nat) : Nat :=
  let rec go : Nat → Nat → Nat
    | 0,     acc => sorry
    | n + 1, acc => sorry
  go n 1

def filter (p : α → Bool) (xs : List α) : List α :=
  let rec go : List α → List α → List α
    | [],      acc => sorry
    | x :: xs, acc => sorry
  go xs []

end Tail

#eval Tail.length   (List.range 10000)   -- shouldn't overflow
#eval Tail.factorial 6                   -- expect: 720
#eval Tail.filter (· > 5) [1, 6, 3, 8]   -- expect: [6, 8]
